places.name: Hotel Gracery Shinjuku
places.note: Big chain hotel in Kabukicho. Convenient base for first leg in Tokyo — a few minutes walk from Shinjuku Station.

places.name: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo
places.note: Used for the final stretch in Tokyo. West Shinjuku, near the Metro Government Building and the limousine bus stop for Narita.

places.name: Shibuya Crossing
places.note: Yes, do it. Best viewed from above — the Starbucks at Tsutaya or the Shibuya Sky observation deck.

places.name: Meiji Shrine
places.note: Forested shrine just behind Harajuku. Quiet, gravel paths, big torii. Go in the morning.

places.name: Imperial Palace
places.note: The grounds are free to walk; the inner palace only opens twice a year. Easy to combine with Tokyo Station.

places.name: Tsukiji Outer Market
places.note: Inner fish auction moved to Toyosu, but the outer market is still the best place to eat your way through breakfast.

places.name: Tokyo City View & Sky Deck
places.note: Roppongi Hills observatory. Open-air sky deck on top — the only one in Tokyo where you’re actually outside.

places.name: teamLab Borderless
places.note: Immersive digital art. Originally at Odaiba; relocated to Azabudai Hills in 2024. Book ahead.

places.name: Tokyo DisneySea
places.note: If you only do one Disney park ever, do this one. Exists nowhere else. Full day, weekday if possible.

places.name: Odaiba
places.note: Reclaimed island in Tokyo Bay — Rainbow Bridge views, oversized shopping malls, a Gundam statue.

places.name: Shimo-Kitazawa
places.note: Indie bohemian neighborhood west of Shibuya — thrift shops, tiny cafes, live music. Worth a slow afternoon.

places.name: Ginza
places.note: Tokyo’s polished shopping district. Streets close to cars on weekend afternoons.

places.name: Harajuku & Omotesando
places.note: Pair Takeshita Street’s teen pop chaos with the tree-lined Omotesando boulevard for a high–low afternoon.

places.name: Sushi Bar Yasuda
places.note: Minato omakase counter by the chef who made his name at Sushi Yasuda NYC. Reserve weeks ahead.

places.name: Shinjuku Warayakiya
places.note: Straw-fired bonito (katsuo no tataki). Big portions, casual, in Shinjuku.

places.name: Nakaya (Tsukiji)
places.note: Tsukiji outer market spot — stop for a fresh sashimi breakfast.

places.name: Totti Candy Factory
places.note: Rainbow cotton candy on Takeshita Street, Harajuku. Touristy and absurd but the photo.

places.name: mois cafe
places.note: Tiny neighborhood cafe in Shimo-Kitazawa — good pit stop while wandering.

places.name: Samurai Restaurant (ex-Robot)
places.note: Kabukicho’s legendary neon-and-robots dinner show. Note: the original closed during COVID — the Samurai Restaurant successor reopened in roughly the same venue.

places.name: Laforet Club Hakone Gora
places.note: Onsen hotel in Gora. Soak, kaiseki dinner, sleep on futon. The whole point of a Hakone stop.

places.name: Hakone (region)
places.note: Mountain town two hours from Tokyo — hot springs, open-air museums, and Mt. Fuji views on a clear day. Use the Hakone Free Pass for the loop.

places.name: The Royal Park Hotel Kyoto Sanjo
places.note: Central Kyoto, steps from Pontocho and the Kamogawa river. Walking distance to Gion.

places.name: Fushimi Inari Taisha
places.note: The thousand torii gates. Free, open 24/7. Go very early (before 8am) or after sunset to skip the crowds.

places.name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
places.note: The gold-leaf temple by the pond. Quick visit — 30 minutes is plenty — pair it with Ryoan-ji next door.

places.name: Ryoan-ji
places.note: Famous Zen rock garden. Sit on the wooden veranda for ten minutes and just look.

places.name: Kiyomizu-dera
places.note: Wooden temple on stilts above the city. The approach through Higashiyama old town is half the experience.

places.name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
places.note: Western edge of Kyoto. Get there at sunrise — it’s short and gets shoulder-to-shoulder fast.

places.name: Arashiyama Monkey Park Iwatayama
places.note: Climb up the hill above the bamboo grove for wild macaques and a panoramic view of Kyoto. ~30 min hike.

places.name: Nishiki Market
places.note: Five-block covered food street downtown. Pickles, sweets, sashimi sticks, knives. Great for grazing lunch.

places.name: Pontocho Alley
places.note: Narrow lantern-lit lane of restaurants along the Kamogawa. Walk it at dusk; pick somewhere with a riverside terrace in season.

places.name: Gion
places.note: The old geisha district — wooden machiya houses, lantern light. Be a respectful tourist; no chasing geiko for photos.

places.name: Yamafuku (shabu-shabu)
places.note: Pontocho-area shabu-shabu. Reservation recommended.

places.name: Kikunoi Roan
places.note: Roan is the Pontocho outpost of the Michelin-starred Kikunoi — contemporary kaiseki. Reserve well ahead.

places.name: Yaruki Yakiniku
places.note: Casual yakiniku grill-your-own in central Kyoto. Good after a long temple day.

places.name: Nineizaka Sushi Numamoto
places.note: Quiet intimate sushi counter on Ninenzaka in Higashiyama. Reserve.

places.name: Maiko Evening Performance
places.note: Yasakadori Enraku puts on an early-evening maiko dinner show — a chaperoned, beginner-friendly way to actually see one perform. Booked through Veltra.

places.name: Private Kyoto Day Tour
places.note: A 6-hour private guided day (booked via ToursByLocals) covered Kinkaku-ji, Ryoan-ji, and Kiyomizu-dera. Worth it on day one to get your bearings.

places.name: L’Escamoteur Bar
places.note: Dim, theatrical cocktail bar just off Kawaramachi-dori. Excellent nightcap.

places.name: Nara Park
places.note: Day trip from Kyoto (~45 min). 1,200 free-roaming deer that bow for crackers. Combine with Todai-ji on the same walk.

places.name: Todai-ji
places.note: The world’s largest wooden building, housing the world’s largest bronze Buddha. Inside Nara Park.

places.name: Kasuga Taisha
places.note: Famous lantern shrine inside Nara Park — thousands of bronze and stone lanterns along a wooded approach.

places.name: Fraser Residence Nankai Osaka
places.note: Apartment-style hotel in Namba. Steps from Dotonbori and the Nankai line to Koyasan.

places.name: Dotonbori
places.note: Osaka’s neon canal-side eating street — takoyaki, okonomiyaki, the Glico running man. Best at night.

places.name: Kuromon Ichiba Market
places.note: Osaka’s “kitchen” — 600m of stalls selling A5 wagyu skewers, uni, fresh oysters. Go hungry.

places.name: Ichiran Dotonbori (Honkan)
places.note: The 24-hour tonkotsu chain’s flagship branch in Dotonbori. Order from a vending machine, eat in a solo booth.

places.name: Koyasan Syukubo Ekoin
places.note: Working Shingon temple that hosts overnight guests. Vegetarian shojin-ryori dinner, morning fire ceremony, and the cemetery night tour leaves from here. Highlight of the trip.

places.name: Okunoin Cemetery
places.note: Japan’s largest cemetery — 200,000+ moss-covered tombs under towering cedars, leading to the mausoleum of Kobo Daishi. Walk it at night with Ekoin’s monk-led tour, then again at sunrise.

places.name: Koyasan (Mt. Koya)
places.note: Mountain-top monastic town founded 1,200 years ago. Access via the Nankai line from Osaka + cable car. Plan one overnight.
